For example, if the target is "code", each copy requires one 'c', one 'o', one 'd', and one 'e'. So for each character in the target, I need to know how many times it appears. Then, for each of those characters in s, I divide the count in s by the count required in the target. The minimum of all those divisions gives the maximum number of copies possible.

4. For each character in the target, divide the count in s by the count required in the target. Take the floor of that division. The minimum of all these values across all characters in the target is the maximum number of copies possible.

#include <unordered_map>

class Solution {
public:
    int rearrangeCharacters(string s, string target) {
        unordered_map<char, int> targetFreq;
        unordered_map<char, int> sFreq;

        for (char c : target) {
            targetFreq[c]++;
        }

        for (char c : s) {
            sFreq[c]++;
        }

        int res = INT_MAX;
        for (auto& pair : targetFreq) {
            char c = pair.first;
            if (sFreq.find(c) == sFreq.end()) {
                return 0;
            } else {
                int required = pair.second;
                int available = sFreq[c];
                res = min(res, available / required);
            }
        }

        return res;
    }
};

solution:  
```cpp  
class Solution {  
public:  
    int rearrangeCharacters(string s, string target) {  
        unordered_map<char, int> targetFreq;  
        unordered_map<char, int> sFreq;  
  
        for (char c : target) {  
            targetFreq[c]++;  
        }  
  
        for (char c : s) {  
            sFreq[c]++;  
        }  
  
        int res = INT_MAX;  
        for (auto& pair : targetFreq) {  
            char c = pair.first;  
            if (sFreq.find(c) == sFreq.end()) {  
                return 0;  
            } else {  
                int required = pair.second;  
                int available = sFreq[c];  
                res = min(res, available / required);  
            }  
        }  
  
        return res;  
    }  
};  
```  
efficiency: HIGH  
time complexity: O(N + M) where N is the length of `s` and M is the length of `target`  
space complexity: O(1) (bounded by fixed-size character sets)  
energy implications: LOW  
explanation: The approach counts character frequencies in both `s` and `target`, then computes the maximum number of `target` copies by taking the minimum ratio of available characters to required ones. Strengths include linear time efficiency and simplicity. Weaknesses involve extra space for frequency maps, though this is negligible due to fixed-size character sets.
